codeSnapshot.label: train_val_test_split.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedShuffleSplit

# Standard random split — 70/15/15
X, y = np.random.randn(10000, 20), np.random.randint(0, 2, 10000)

# Step 1: carve off test set first (never touch again until final eval)
X_trainval, X_test, y_trainval, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.15, random_state=42, stratify=y  # stratify preserves class ratio
)
# Step 2: split remaining 85% into train / val
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X_trainval, y_trainval, test_size=0.15/0.85,  # 15% of original
    random_state=42, stratify=y_trainval
)
print(f'Train: {len(X_train)}, Val: {len(X_val)}, Test: {len(X_test)}')
# → Train: 7000, Val: 1500, Test: 1500

# Stratified split (sklearn StratifiedShuffleSplit)
# Ensures each split has the same class distribution as the full dataset
sss = StratifiedShuffleSplit(n_splits=1, test_size=0.15, random_state=42)
for train_idx, test_idx in sss.split(X, y):
    X_train_strat, X_test_strat = X[train_idx], X[test_idx]
    y_train_strat, y_test_strat = y[train_idx], y[test_idx]

# Temporal split for time series — never use random split for time series!
# Must split chronologically to avoid lookahead bias
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'date': pd.date_range('2020-01-01', periods=10000, freq='h'),
                   'value': np.random.randn(10000)})
df = df.sort_values('date')
train_end = int(0.70 * len(df))
val_end   = int(0.85 * len(df))
df_train  = df.iloc[:train_end]
df_val    = df.iloc[train_end:val_end]
df_test   = df.iloc[val_end:]
print(f'Temporal split — Train: {len(df_train)}, Val: {len(df_val)}, Test: {len(df_test)}')

eli3: Before training a model, you split your data into three groups: training data (to fit the model), validation data (to tune the model's settings), and test data (to get a fair final score). The test set is locked away and only used once — at the very end.

Without this split, you'd be like a student who studies the exam questions directly — you'd get a perfect score but you wouldn't have actually learned anything. The test set simulates new, unseen data and tells you how your model will perform in the real world.

The most important rule: never let the test set influence any training decision. If you use the test set to choose your model or tune hyperparameters, your final accuracy estimate will be optimistically wrong — a common mistake called data leakage.

whenWhy.title: Unbiased performance estimation
whenWhy.body: The train set fits the model. The validation set guides hyperparameter search and model selection. The test set provides a final unbiased estimate of generalisation performance. Without a separate test set, all performance estimates are optimistic because the model (or hyperparameters) were chosen to perform well on that data. Goodfellow et al. (2016) emphasise this as the fundamental protocol: 'Never use information from the test set to make choices about the model or hyperparameters.' Common ratios: 70/15/15 (smaller datasets), 98/1/1 (large datasets where 1% is plenty for evaluation).

whenWhy.title: Stratified splits for imbalanced classification
whenWhy.body: Random splits can leave certain classes under-represented in one split by chance (especially for rare classes with <5% prevalence). Stratified splitting ensures each split has the same class proportion as the full dataset. sklearn.StratifiedShuffleSplit or train_test_split(stratify=y) handles this automatically. Critical for medical datasets (rare disease prevalence 1%) or fraud detection (fraud rate 0.1%) where a non-stratified test set might not contain any rare class examples.

whenWhy.title: Temporal splits for time-series data
whenWhy.body: For time-series data (stock prices, sensor readings, user activity logs), random splits cause data leakage: training on data from after the test period (lookahead bias). The model appears to predict the future but has actually seen it. Always use chronological splits: train on t<T_train, validate on T_train≤t<T_val, test on t≥T_val. Walk-forward validation (expanding or sliding window) is the time-series equivalent of cross-validation.

deepDiveIntro: The three-way split formalizes three distinct roles. Training data fits model parameters. Validation data selects hyperparameters — the chosen hyperparameters are those that minimize validation error. Test data estimates generalization performance, and this estimate is unbiased only if the test set is used exactly once. Every additional look at the test set during development inflates the estimate through implicit multiple comparisons, which is why the test set must be sealed until the final model is ready. [1]

For small datasets, a single fifteen-percent test set may have high variance as an estimator of true error. K-fold cross-validation reduces this variance by averaging over multiple held-out folds. Even so, a dedicated test set should still be reserved for the final evaluation — cross-validation is appropriate for model selection, but the final number reported in a paper or product should come from a test set not used during selection. [1]

deepDive.heading: Split Ratios and Dataset Size
deepDive.body: The right split ratio depends on dataset size. For about a thousand examples, a 60/20/20 split gives roughly two hundred examples per evaluation set, which is marginal but workable. For a hundred thousand examples, 80/10/10 is standard. For ten million, 98/1/1 still leaves a hundred thousand per set — more than enough. The key constraint is that the test set needs enough examples to estimate the target metric with low variance. For a five percent error rate, achieving plus-or-minus half a point of precision requires roughly 7,600 test examples by a Wilson confidence interval calculation. [1]

deepDive.heading: Data Leakage
deepDive.body: Data leakage: any information from the test set (or future data) flowing into the training process. Types: (1) Target leakage — a feature derived from the label is included in training features. (2) Train-test contamination — test examples appear in the training set (e.g., deduplication not performed). (3) Preprocessing leakage — fit a scaler on all data including test, then evaluate on test (inflates performance). Prevention: always fit preprocessing (scalers, PCA, imputers) on training data only, transform val/test using the training statistics. sklearn Pipeline enforces this: fit_transform on train, transform on val/test. [1]

deepDive.heading: Walk-Forward Validation for Time Series
deepDive.body: Time series cross-validation maintains chronological order. The expanding window variant trains on all data up to a cutoff and evaluates on the next window, then extends the cutoff and repeats. The sliding window variant trains on a fixed-length window that advances forward each fold. Both prevent lookahead bias. A gap between the training end and the evaluation start is sometimes necessary when features are computed over trailing windows that would otherwise mix training and evaluation periods. Sklearn's TimeSeriesSplit implements expanding-window cross-validation with a configurable gap parameter. [1]

youtubeLabel: Train, Validation, Test Split — Why and How
takeaways: Train: fit model; Val: tune hyperparameters; Test: final unbiased estimate — never touch test until the end. [1]
Stratified split: preserves class distribution in each set — essential for imbalanced datasets. [1]
Temporal split for time-series: always chronological — random splits cause lookahead bias. [1]
Preprocessing leakage: fit scalers/imputers on train only, transform val/test with training statistics. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the 'test set contamination' problem in LLM benchmark evaluation?
edgeCases.a: LLMs are pre-trained on massive internet corpora that may include benchmark test sets (MMLU, HellaSwag, HumanEval). If the model saw the test questions during pre-training, benchmark scores overestimate true generalisation — a form of train-test contamination at the pre-training stage. Detection: compare model accuracy on questions known to be in the training corpus vs. a held-out set. Mitigation: hold out new benchmarks created after the model's training cutoff (e.g., ARC-Challenge-2024). OpenAI and Google routinely test for contamination by checking n-gram overlap between training data and benchmarks. This is a fundamental challenge for honest LLM evaluation.

edgeCases.q: How does the validation set become 'contaminated' through hyperparameter search?
edgeCases.a: If you run hundreds of hyperparameter configurations and select the one with the best validation error, the validation error becomes an optimistic estimate of generalisation — you've implicitly fit the hyperparameters to the validation set. This is sometimes called 'validation set overfitting' or 'hyperparameter overfitting.' Quantitatively: with k independent configurations tested, the expected minimum validation error is E[min(ε₁,...,εₖ)] < true_error. Solutions: (1) Nested cross-validation (outer loop for test estimate, inner loop for hyperparameter search). (2) Hold out a separate 'meta-test' set after hyperparameter search. (3) Use Bayesian optimisation which is more sample-efficient, reducing the number of configurations tried.

sources.label: Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville (2016) — Deep Learning, Chapter 5 & 11 (MIT Press)
Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman (2009) — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Chapter 7 (Springer)
